Washington, July 27th.
Mr. v Badger commenced his speech by e*.
- pressing his great regret that the bill had been
brought forward j and that it had been brought
forward wiih a notice that It was to be driven
nod pressed through without a fair opportunity
far discussion. The hot haste of the friends of
this blit would ever force senators, in this ex
hausting weather, when they could neither do
justice to themselves nor the subject, to speak
Dilate and unseasonable hours, or relinquish
their right to discuss the question.
The Compromise embraced in the Territorial
Bill will be found upon close examination to be
a virtual surrender by the South of almost all
.that they claim. Ills attempted by the agita
tors to show that it protects slavery—and that
It enlists in aid of slavery the influence or gov
ernment officers and the sympathies of a slave
holding Supreme Court. There is nothing sub
stantial In these objections. We might as well
distrust government officers and the Supreme
Court on any other subject as upon this.
As to Oregon, the bill as proposed to be
amended,—prohibiting the territorial legislature
from establishing slavery without the assent of
Congress—declares it to be in fact and forever
free territory.
Then as to California and New Mexico, how.
is slavery protected there, or in any way en-<
outraged by this bill 1 There 1h no slavery now
existing in those territories, and the new gov
ernment is prohibited from establishing any—
or from making any law on the subject. There
being no local law on the subject, slave proper
ty cannot exist there under the protection of
law. But the bill says to the slave-holders, if.
you claim any rights under the Constitution
which you carry with you as citizens oi the
Union, .which ought to override the local law;
vou may adjudicate the case belore the court
of the" territory,—and either party may, if not
satisfied with the result, appeal to the Supreme
Court. What is there in this for the protection
and extension of slavery! Is the Supreme
Court to decide that, though there is no. local
law for holding slaves, and though there can
be no law establishing slavery by the power of
the general government, and though there is no
treaty stipulation on the subject which con
strains us to recognise African slavery, yel that
it can be established by judicial assumption !
The idea Is preposterous that the Supreme
Court would undertake to establish slavery in
that'mode. When they have made so absurd
a decision it will be time enough to raise a
clamor against them.
But the truth is that the Supreme Court will
decide, and must decide, that they have nothing
to do with the matter.
He adverted to the fact that, after the Senate
had sat eight hours, yesterday, an adjournment
was refused. It was unfortunate, he thought,
that a bill, the professed object of which was to
assuage excitement and produce conciliation,
should be urged in such a temper. Upon this,
more thau any other subject, it was proper that
Senators should have an opportunity to speak
fully and deliberate at leisure.
The bill was urged because it was said it
would allay excitement. But bow was exccite-
menl io be allayed by forced measures. It must
be allayed only by no appeal to reason—to tbe
calm judgment of the public. '
As to this bill, be was opposed to it, because
it surrendered every thing on the part of tbe
South.
He went on to argue against the power cf
Congress to Impose any restriction upon the
territories—that they could not legislate for the
exclusion nor for the introduction of slavery.
He contended that the Southern men had the
right to carry slaves into ihe territory; and that
the law for the protection of slavery sprung up
as a consequence of that right.
Tbe Supreme Court, as he proceeded to show,
• would be obliged tc decide that the U. Stales
f overoment bad nothing to do with tbe question.
le cited in support of this opinion, the opin
ions of the Southern Senator* who opposed the
Missouri restriction. The distinguished Wil
liam Pinckney, of Md., gave it as his opinion
that slavery could only exift by the statutory
enactment of me local government,—and his
doctrine- was, therefore, that Congress could'
impose no restriction on Missouri, nor in any
way interfere with the question.
Tbe South, he insisted, by this bill made a total
srurenderof tbe whole question. Whatson of a
compromise was it to give up the whole subject
matter in dispute. He would rather, at once,
give up all negro slavery. How was tbe honor
of the South saved by this question 1 It sub
mitted, the right to the decision of an arbiter
that we knew must decide the question against
He could see nothing that we were to gain
by this bilh Would it quiet agitation! We
had no reasonable assurance that it would end
this agitation. If he coaid believe that, he
would vote for the bill, and go among his peo
ple aud appeal to them to give their assent to it.
But the moment this bill passed, it was to be
the signal for a more general and dangerous
agitation or the subject. If there was any part
of Ibis new territory which was adapted to the
cultivation of cotton, &c., he wished the South
to have tbe benefit of it; or, if they relinquish it,
he wished it to be for some great object—some
national good.
There was a sort of understanding in the Sen
ate last night, that the-question on the compro
mise bill, should be taken to day. A motion
was made by Mr. Hannegan to take a©recess
from 4 to 5 o’clock, in order to return here and
sit out tbe bill to-night. Mr. Benton objected,
it was bad enough to sit here during Hog-days,
let alone dog-nights, Mr. Upbam wanted to
speak bat could'not, in his feeble health, be
said, speak at night. In fact, Ihe proposition
was unacceptable and was withdrawn.
•The friends of the bill were the more anxious
to get the vote, for it was evident that the meas
ure- was losing strength and would gain none,
and there was danger that the irritation grow
ing out of the discussion would alienate the
support both of some Northern and Southern
men. It was also perceived that no two indi
viduals, whether in Tavor of or against the bill,
could agree upon the construction of a single
important provision of it.
- The more the subject was examined the more
complicated and difficult did it seem to become,
and those who were disposed to vole for the bill,
and did vote for |t, were becoming very indiffer
ent to. its passage. Another week s debate
would have caused the rejection of the bill..
Tbe House has (be same subject also before
f them. Tbelr Oregon Bill, which is now under
consideration,iso bill to establish a territory
over Oregon, and 'embracing a provision for
the extension of the ordinance or 1787 to the
.'■•territory. The debate turns upon the question
of the power ol Congress to inhibit slavery in
with a single objection, with this bill, and will
ing to submit tho question to tbe supreme
Court or tbe United States, as it will be free
from {those influences and prejudices which
govern men elsewhere.
Mr. Bradbury made a few remarks directly
to the point, when, shortly ofter one o’clock, an
ineffectual effort was made to adjourn. Mr.
Bradbury appeared to think, and very properly,
that where the South supposed tho North had
gained everything, and the North that the South
had gained every thing, there was danger that
one party would be disappointed. And be did
not believe that the passage of the bill would be
calculated to allay excitement. He pointed
out one or two delects in the bill, in reference
to the habeas corpus, which he deemed it ne
cessary to remedy. He denied that the North
had manifested an aggressive spirit toward the
South, or a disposition to pursue on aggressive
policy, and replied at some length to the posi
tions assumed by several Senators. The im
pression drawn from his remarks was, that he
intends to vote against the bill, though not for
sectional reasons, for he deprecated all section
al organization.
Mr. Niles obtained the floor at a quarter past
two and said the Senate appeared to be govern
ed very much by the spirit ul the Compromise
Committee who reported the bill. He did not
intend at this early hour of the-day, bnt he
thoucht it his duty, to occupy tbe attention of
the Senate, or the attention of the Chair, for
Senators appeared Jo have other employment,
for a short time, perhaps not more than a cou
ple of hours.
Ho had no idea of hurrying through this
great question, this bill, this miserable contri
vance. This bill was not only negatively bad,
bill in regard to two of these territories, he fell
it his duty, in the face of this Senate—there
was not much of a Senate here, but in the face
ol the American people—to denounce this bill
as a flagrant violation ol the rights which we
had guaranteed to them—the right to be taken
into the Union as citizens, whenever, we could
do so consiliutinal'.y. But instead of this, we
were sending to them pro consuls aud Cice
ro* to exercise the powers of government over
a people two thousand miles off. And in reler-
ence to Oregon, which had declared its will
upon the subject, we had given her three months
grace, belore abrogating her law prohibiting
slavery. There was something significant a-
bout this, fie considered the laws of Oregon
as con)ing here with binding foice. Gentlemen
here seemed to think that the constitution is a
law, and if it only goes then it regulates every
thing. But it is no such thing.
The constitution only defines the character
of legislation—the restrictions applicable to pri
vate rights. When organizing these territories
we should leave them as we find them, to make
such regulation* and pass such law*, notlncon-
sis.em with the constitution, as-they may de
sire. He expressed the believe that some sort
of trick had been resorted to—an intention to
hold this bill out to the community as some
thing, when it was really nothing. If there
was an honest intention that this question should
be carried up to the Supreme Court, then Sen
ators ought to be gratified at amendments in
tended to facilitate the so carrying of it up.—
He spoke until 3 A. M., when he gave way
for the yeas and nays to be again called on
a motion to adjourn, and the motion was a-
gain decided in the negative—ayes 4, nays 30.
lie accordingly proceeded with his remarks,
as he safd, with renewed vigor, after the short
respite which had been given him. From
the arguments heie, he said, he supposed it was
the natural right of mau to be born slaves.—
Others had supposed that all men had been
born, or treated, as the Senator from South
Carolina had it, free and equal. But this, from
tbe able arguments made here, was evidently a
mistake, &c. He concluded abont 4 A. M..
when
Mr. Atchision look the floor and continued
the debate until 5, when Mr. Bright addressed
the Senate at length on the same subject. He
thanked heaven that while the corrupt and cor
roded spirit of demagogneUm was at work in
New York, Indiana was safe.
Wr. Walker presented the resolution* and
instructions of the Wisconsin Legislature,
against tbe extension of slavery, and expressed
his intention to obey those instructions, both

jections to the bill in several particulars, and

Washington, July, 19,1848.
Gentlemen:—In the year of our .Lord 1837,
the Congress of the United Stales Constituted a
select Committee of nine, with plenary powers,
“to investigate the Execotive Departments.”—
That Committee was lomnosed of Henry A.
Wise, (Chairman) Messrs.Pearce, of Rhode Isl
and, Mulenberg, Campbell, Hannegan, Parks,
Lincoln, Mann and Chaney.
From the Journal of that Committee, House
Reps. No. 194, pp. 15 and 16, 1 extract tbe
following:
Mr-Wise beitig duly sworn, was asked by
Mr. Mann, of New York—"Do you of your
own knowledge know of any act by either of
the beads of the Executive Departments which
is either corrupt or a violation of their official
duties 1”
[I regret that it is not in my power to send
you a copy of the Journal.]
Mr. Wise goes on to answer in detail, and
states:
“I believe that Lewis Cass, Secretary of
War, was engaged inspecnlaijog in the public
lands, whilst Secretary of War; that he made
exorbitant allowances to favorites; paid one for
services never performed; another, after he bad
full knowedge that the favorite had lorged bis
official signature; permitted commissions under
him to be ante dated; and has ordered a treasu
ry warrant to be paid to the assignee of a dis
bursing officer who bad gambled it away, after
it had been protested by a deposile bank, and
was countermanded by the Secretary of the
Treasury; and to have been guilty of several
other acts ot violation of duly.”
“I believe that the wars with the Creek and
Seminole Indians have been caused by want o(
integrity in Executive officers of tbe Govern
ment; and that the defeat of oor arms, which
has covered the nation with disgrace in these
wars has beeif owing to neglect or want of abil-
ty in the War Department.”
These extracts are verbatim el leleralim, el
punctualim, and I submit them to the country
without comment. Mr. Wise had no doubt in
vestigated the subject thoroughly, and spoke
upon evidence satisfactory to his mind.

Bible Presented Co Jacksonville Division.
Bro. Gorman : 1 he bons in Unslow are laooring
zealously in the cause of Temperance. Jacksonville
Division is in a very flourishing condition, and tho'
yet in its infancy numbers thirty-three members, and
increasing rapidly several initiations at every meet
ing.
At 3 o'clock. P. M.. on Mondav the 24th-of June.
the Sons belonging to Jacksonville Division, visited
by those of the neighboring Divisions, Richlands and
Tuckahoe, assembled at their Division Room, and
after making necessary arrangements for a proces-
tuon, all in full regalia, marched to the Court House,
where, after prayer by Rev. J. H. Jefferson, and an
ode by the Sons, the Rev. J. M. Sharpe delivered an
eloquent and appropriate address to a very large and
interesting auuicnce.
The address being concluded, the Ladies, through
their committee, Misses Mary E. Cox, Maria L
Simmons and Julia A. M. Cook, presented a Bible to
the Division.
Migs iSimmons arose and introduced Miss Lox as
donor, who, in making the present, made the follow
ing remarks :
W orthy Patriarch, Officers and Members of Jackson
ville Division of the Sons of lemperance :
We are here presented to you on this interesting
occasion, as the Committer for the Ladies, who are
your humble co-laborers inthis noble cause, to pre
sent this Bible as a token of our regard for you. This
is a precious volume it leads to truth and happiness,
for it is God's holy oracle. May it not only be pe
rused by one, but by all of you, and may all who read
it, be benetmed by yielding to its sweet influence.
un : would that 1 couia nnd language to express
the feelings of those I represent in proffering this
gift ! . Mothers and daughters, do you not feel proud
to see your fathers and husbands, your brothers and
sons, engaged in this glorious work, by resisting that
poisonous alcohol, which not only tends to destroy
the health but the mind, after a short period of time?
Oh ! what a happy change to see them, instead of
participating in that poisonous draught, step forward
and take a drink of the pure, cool and sparkling wa
ter. Receive now this gift, and may all who feel inter
ested in this great, noble and glorious cause, be the
children of light.
And may their paths be decorated with the sweet
est of all flowers, Temperance, Religion, Love, Puri
ty and Fidelity.
Messrs. Frederick Foy and Robert F. Williams, on
the part of the Sons, requested Col. L. W. Humphrey
to receive the gift. Accordingly Col. Humphrey a
rose and said :
Miss Cox: We are proud to recognize you and
the Ladies whom you represent as co-laborers in the
noble cause which we have espoused.
This inestimable gift, as a testimony of respect for
our Fraternity, and the tribute you have just paid
to our Order while presenting it, have excited in us
emotions the most lively and cheering.
You have been pleased to speak of us engaged in a
noble cause : the cause is a noble one : it is our cause,
it is your cause, which has for its aim the aggran
dizement of our fellow-man ; yes, it is the cause of all
mankind.
Our efforts in this glorious work of reformation my
brothers, have not been unobserved but rightly ap
preciated by the Ladies for see this precious vol
ume, the Bible, their token of regard for us, which I
receive for you, with ineffable delight, and be assur
ed Miss Cox and Ladies, I shall deliver it to them
with heartfelt gratitude.
And my brothers, having for our motto " Love,
Purity and Fidelity" our guide, this Bible our co
laborers, the Ladies, we will, with an increased cour
age, dare meet the stoutest foe that ever raised his
voioe in the ranks of Bacchus.
Yes my brothers,
" Let this be our guide, disdain nmbition's call,
And if wo fall by this, we nobly fall."
An Ode by the Sons, the company dispersed, and
they returned to their Lodge, and immediately twelve
petitions were presented, ballotted for, and received
by the Division.
The 24th of June was an unusual day for Jack
sonville, and from circumstances connected, a day
long to be remembered.
It was St. John's Day, and the Masons of Lafay
ette Lodge celebrated it.
At 11 o'clock, A. M., they formed a procession,
and marched in front of the Division room, when the
dons joined in tormmg a very large and imposing
procession.
They then marched back to the Court House, where
they were entertained by the ltev. J. H. Jefferson, in
an able, elomicntand annronriate speech, after which
all were invited by the Masons and Sons, to partake
with them of a dinner, prepared by Dr. Cook for the
occasion, in which about three hundred persons par
ticipated.
The ceremonies of the day were well conducted,
and all seemed highly delighted. Messrs. W. P.
Ward and F. Harrison of Zion's Lodge, were present,
with whose assistance many persons were initiated.
Richland Division is prospering, their number is
sixteen. You see Bro. Gorman, our work in Onslow
goes bravely on. Yours in L. P. & F.

Ralii and Heat In the United States.
The last Patent Office report contains meteorolo
gical tables and statistics, by which we learn the
mean annual depth of rain at various places in the
United States, as well as the mean annual tcm
perature. The greatest depth of rain falls at Fort
Fort Monroe, Va., where it is 52.53 inches ; the least
at the St. Louis Arsenal, Mo., where it is 24.12 inch
es, or less than half. At Fort Hamilton, N. 1
tho depth is 45.71 ; and, at West Point, 48.70. As
a general rule, the least rain falls in the Western
States : and the most on tho Atlantic sea-coast, and
on the Eastern side of mountain ranges. At New
Orleans, however, and other points near the Gulf of
Mexico, the fall is heavy it being 51.85 at New Or
leans. The fall at Boston and Baltimore is about
equal being 39.23 at the former place, and 39.90
at the latter. Through Michigan and Missouri, it
ranges at about 30.00 ; but. as we go more South, it
rises; and, in Arkansas, averages 35.00. At Fort
Constitution, N. H.,-the depth is 28.85 the lowest
on the Atlantic sea-board. At Key West, Fa., the
fall is comparatively slight, also being but 31.49
At Charleston, S. C, it "is 33.89. At Washington,
D. C, 34.o2 : and, from that place, it steadily m
creases as it goes North, till it reaches West Point,
where it begins to decline.
The mean annual heat of the cities of the United
States exhibits somewhat different results. The
most southern parts of course are the hottest ; the
sea-ports, in the same latitude, are cooler than inland
towns. The hottest city appears to be Jakson
Mississippi, where the average temperature is 65.6
of Farenheit : the coolest is the little town of Penn
Yan, in New York, which is 45.46. The tables do
not include Texas, however, in the south, or Maine.
New Hampshire, or Vermont, in the north. Cam
bridge, Masachusettes, is a comparatively cool iown
the average temperature being 47.48 : while that ol
Philadelphia is 52.57. New Haven, Connecticut, is
49.00; New Jersey, 50.89; Louisville, Kentucky,
53.08 : and Fort Madison, Iowa, 49.62. These, be
it remembered, are the average temperatures. Some
of the towns mentioned above as comparatively hot
have summers unusually cool for their location
while others with a low average temperature, are
excessively sultry in July and August.
Ascending on Horseback in a Balloon. All Pa
ris was agog on the 7th instant, to see a man ascend
in a balloon on horseback. 1 he horse, a hne and
spirited young white one, was suspended beneath the
balloon, in the place usually occupied by the car.
Bands passed beneath the belly and well secured, left
the animal in an easy position, with the legs free.
M. Poitevin, clothed as a Jockey, mounted the horse,
which was saddled and bridled in the ordinary man
ner, and gave orders to cut loose ! The horse seem
ed loth to quit his mother earth, and remonstrated a
. little when he found that he was being taken off his
: feet. But once in the air he became as motionless as
though he bad been struck with paralysis.
SPEECH OF HON. AV. S. ASHE,
Ob tli Question of tKe AdmUslen of tle Delegates
from 17 tab and New Mexico, delivered lu tlie House
or Representative. July 16th, 1850,
Mr. Ashe being entitled to the floor, said :
Mr, Chairman, the excessive and almost insuffer
able warmth of the weather must be taken as my
apology for the few and incoherent remarks which I
will otter to the House on the matter now penaing
before it. As one of the Committee on Elections, l
can now say that this application from New Mexico
received at their hands a most sedulons examination.
I agree entirely with the majority in the report and
the accompanying resolutions submitted by them,
and will further say, that any other conclusion on
their part would not only have been incompatible
with the usages and practices of Congress, but in di
rect antagonism with the constitution of the United
States. In making this broad declaration, I intend
to cast no censure on the minority, who made a con
trary report. We have all to abide the workings of
our respective judgments. The minority thought in
one way, reasoned in one way, and concluded in that
way. The majority, actuated by a desire no less
fervent to discharge their duty properly and correct
ly, from the same facts deduced opposite and contra
ry conclusions. As the chairman, in his speech and
report, has most ably argued the merits of the case,
I think it is idle for me to make any extended re
marks. I will, therefore, be brief. In order to determine
correctly the relation which New Mexico bears to
the Union, we should previously examine the relation
which subsisted between New Mexico and Old Mex
ico previous to our taking possession of it. We have
good and indisputable authority for the position that
there were nineteen States composing the Mexican
confederacy, anterior to the separation of Texas from
that confederacy. New Mexico was not recognized
as one of these nineteen States, but, on the contrary,
was considered as a territory attached to Coahuila
and Texas. Its settlement was, indeed, much ante
rior in time to either of them, but it never possessed
the capacity to emerge trom a territorial condition.
This fact is not at all impugned by the consideration
that this territory was possessed of a Provincial Coun
cil, invested with legislative powers ; for its legisla
tion merely extended to municipal regulations. We
should bear in mind that Coahuila and Texas form
ed one of the Mexican States ; the first-named lying
on the west of the Rio Grande : the last, Texas, ly
ing on the east of that river. As early as the year
loz7, there was an attempt made to establish a sep
arate and independent Republic, called Fredonia,
composed of Coahuila and Texas. If that attempt
nad been successful, would not INew Mexico, as at
tached territory, have followed in the wake I I am
clearly of that opinion. But should New Mexico, in
the event of the Texan revolution, be considered as
an incident of Coahuila on the west, or of Texas on
the east of the Rio Grande t (I of course refer only
to that portion of New Mexico lying east of the Rio
Grande) The last is her true position. The diplo
matic history of the United States will throw consid
erable light on this point. By the treaty of 1803,
by which we acquired from France, Louisiana, it was
carefully stipulated that all the rights in their fullest
extent resulting fb France from the treaty of Ilde
fonso, made between France and Spain, should ac
crue to the United States, and under that provision
our Government did not hesitate to claim all the
country lying cast of the Rio Grande. This was cer
tainly the opinion not only of Air. Jefferson, but of
every other distinguished statesman of that day ; and
it was in accordance with the correctness of this
claim on our part, that, when we conveyed away
Texas to Spain, as a part of the consideration of the
purchase of Florida, both of the high contracting
parties (Spain and the United States) adopted and
made the Red river as the proper boundary between
the two countries as far west as 100 dee. of loneitudc
west of London, and from thence due north to the
Arkansas river, following the course of the Arkansas
to its source in 42 deg. of latitude, thence bv that
parallel of latitude to the South Sea. What country
were we engaged in conveying away ? Was it not
Texas ? What country, then, did .nese boundaries
circumscribe ? Was it not Texas If this country,
so described, was not a part of Texas, but a posses
sion of Spain, why the particularity of this bounda
ry ? I apprehend it would have been much more
convenient, as well as more consistent with the usa-
es of nations, to have established a common line of
emarcation until the possession of Spain was arri
ved at, and from thence to have made the boundary
of such possessions the dividing line between the two
countries ; and the deviation from that usage in this
case clearly demonstrates that the country socircum-
sciibed and bounded, lying east of the Kio Grande,
was not, in the estimation of either of the contracting
parties, actually appurtenant to the Crown of Spain.
Then, Mr. Speaker, I will submit, that if the rieht
of Spain to this country was acauired from the Uni
ted States by the retrocession of Texas, whether the
onus probandi of a disconnection between Texas and
New Mexico, by Spanish authority, does not devolve
upon the shoulders of those who maintain that when
we reacquired Texas we lost New Mexico? Where
is the act of Spanish authority making this discon
nection ? I have never seen it.
It cannot be pretended that the State of Texas, ei
ther as a component part of the Mexican confedera
cy, or at any time since, ever consented to such a
disconnection. On the contrary, Mr. Speaker, do
we not find her, not only during the misery and trials
of her revolution, but after the achievement of her
independence, after the "lone star" had assumed her
appropriate position in the galaxy of nations, firmly,
stoutly, unceasingly maintaining and asserting her
right to this country1? Texas never relinquished
her rights, and indeed the supposition that any such
relinquishment ever took place is refuted by a suc
cession of legislative acts, all conspiring to reduce
into actual possession, this, her territorial dependen
cy. The last resounding echo of the battle of San

EIGHT VOTES ON THE ELEVENTH, WITHOUT

wasniscTox, Thursday, May 14, 1868, 
The Managers of Impeaghment on the part of the 
House, in view of the strange rumors which have 
been circulated about certain public men regarding 
impeachment, and of the suspicious comment of some 
of the officials of the White House and the clerks 
and relatives of two or three Senators, have deemed 
it their duty to investigate the subject, and to that 
end have issued many subpenas. Among the persons 
gammoned before them are Wm, M. Evarts of the 
President's counsel ; W. W. Warden of‘ the Presi- 
dent’s staff; Senator Trombull’s son; Edwin 
Stevens, Clerk of the House District of Co- 
lumbia Committee, and Mr. Forrest, correspondent 
of The Chicago Times. Several other gentlemen who 
have made themselves more or less conspicuous since 
the trial began, in betting on the vote of some of the 
Senators and predicting that Messrs. Fessenden, 
Trumbull, and Grimes would oppose conviction, are 
also to be summoned. The Managers will require 
them to make known the grounds on which, for 
weeks before the trial, they hazarded thousands of 
dollars on the result. Mr. Stevens and Mr. Trum- 
bull's son have been particularly noticeable in their 
confidence of aoquittal, and it is suspected that 
they are in possession of facts which will threw 
some light upon the defection of the Senators above- 
named. Mr. Johnson remarked to the correspond- 
ent of a New-York Democratic newspaper, last Sun- 
day night, that Fessenden would vote for his aequit- 
tal; which interesting intelligence was published in 
New-York on Monday morning. Fesfenden, on that 
day, in the secret session, justified the prediction 
of his Excellency, Grimes's clerk wagered large 
sums in the Remate lobby on Monday that 
Grimes would go against all the articles, and it 
is well known that Trumbull’s son has won $5,000 on 
the apostacy of his father. The Managers will meet 
to-morrow and take testimony on the subject, and it 
is altogether probable that some important develop- 
ments may be made. The operations of the Whisky 
Ring, too, in securing votes for acquittal, are to be 
vestigated and scrutinized. 
The Senate was not in session to-day, and few of 
the Senators visited the Capitol. No committee 
work of any eonsequence is being transacted, im- 
peachment absorbing almost the sole attention of 
Senators. The excitement and anxiety in regard to 
the resnlt still continne. No one seemingly thinks 
or speaks of anything else. Both sides talk, and ap- 
pear confident of winning. Among the betting men, 
the odds to-day are mostly in favor of acquit- 
tal. It might be proper here to explain 
that Mr. Johnson's most confidential and inti- 
mate friends, those who are with him continu- 
ally, are of that class who do a good deal 
in the betting line, and they appear very sanguine 
of their ecompanion’s acquittal. An attaché of the 
President's staff is known to have some very heavy 
bets pending. He was willing, in fact very anxious, 
to bet on acgnittal days before the trial ended. 
Several days belore Fessenden, Grimes, Trumbull, 
and Henderson made public the conrse which they 
intended to pursue, this individual was ready to 
bet that these very men would vote as they 
have indicated. A very careful canvass of 
Senators has been made by some of the best 
men of the House, and they think that 
the Senate will surely conviet Mr. Johnson 
on two of the articles, and possibly on three, They 
count 38 votes for the eleventh article, without Mr. 
Wade, and 36 for the second-article, with Mr. Wade, 
and the same number for the eighth article. Trum- 
bull. Fessenden, and Grimes are recorded against 
every article. Henderson is expected to vote for the 
eleventh article, althongh some of Lis friends still 
doubt him. Ilis proposition to resign, it is under- 
stood, will not be insisted on, though his effer not to 
vote may be accepted. He agreed to resign if his 
colleagues in the House could get the Governor of 
Missouri to appoint one of the Missouri members who 
understands the whole case in his place, but this it is 
thought would be objected to by the Democrats, and 
it is not to be pressed. Fowler, it is believed, is 
hopelessly gone; yet some of his friends say that he 
will vote for the eleventh article. He is not de- 
. pended on, however, in the canvass. Ross intimates 
that he will vote for the first, second, third, and 
eleventh articles, and may be for the eighth. Fre- 
linghuysen is counted on voting for the same articles 
that Ross will vote for. Willey is put down asin 
favor of the second and eleventh articles, while his 
colleague, Van Winkle, is looked upon as doubt- 
fal. Yet he has said that he will vore 
for the eleventh article, and may do so. 
This disposes of all the doubtful Senators, with the 
exception of Anthony and Sprague. No one 
seems to know anything definite about either of 
these men. Sprague, however, is put down as in fa- 
vor of conviction on four of the articles, while no 
one will venture on Anthony for more than two. 
On the other hand, Messrs. Johnson, Seward, 
Johnny Coyle, and Evarts, figure out seven Repabli- 
ces sure for (acquittal. They are Messrs. Grimes, 
Fessenden, Trumbull, Henderson, Fowler, Van Win- 
kle, and Anthony. They ate also willing to lay 
wagers on Sprague being with them. Several pain 
ful rumors are in circulation about the Rhode Island 
Senators, but as they are merely rumors 1 will not 
give them. 
There is a diversity of opinion about further delay. 
The President’s friends seem to court it, although 
they pretend to wish immediate action. The Repub- 
licans, on the other hand, seem to be unanimous for 
no further postponement. They are anxious to end 
the impeachment and go on with legislative busi- 
ness. Should the Senators whe are ill be unable to 
attend on Satwday their friends ‘will make an effort 
to sccuie a pair for them. This will be difficult, 
shonld it come up. Senator Conkling is rapidly im- 
proving and is expected to be out to-morrow. Sen- 
ator Howard's also’convalescent, but it is still doubt- 
fol whether he will be able to venture out on Satur- 
day. Senator Grimes is no better, and his physicians 
say that be will require very careful treatment. His 
right side still remains paralyzed and is severely 
painful. 
The telegraphic dispatch to The New-York Herald 
about Mr. Colfax having called on Mr. Wade and 
proposed conditions on which he could support ha- 
peachment, is utterly and scandalonsly untrue. Mr. 
Colfax has Leen since February last openly, carnest- 
Jy, and unconditionally for Impeachment; has op- 
posed all postponements of the trial, and uniformly 
said that the speedy ousting of Johnson is worth 
more to the country than all the Vice-Presidential 
nominations to be made this yeay. 
Senator Morton is indispesed, but not serionsly ill. 
16 wonld appear that two-thirds of the Senators are 
worn out by the fatigue of the trial and thy mental 
excitement and anxiety attendant thereon. Whether 
the trial be ended or not on Saturday, it is undoer- 
stood that both honses will adjourn over until after 
the Chicago Conventlon. ‘This will give the large 
namber of the members of the House who desire an 
opportunity to visit Chicago, and at the same time 
allow the Senators a little rest. 
Numerons letters and dispatches from all over the 
country continue to pour in npon members, demand. 
ing conviction. To publish them all would require 
several columns of THe Trisune. Asan evidence 
of {he confidence that friends of conviction have in 
the result, it may be said that George Wilkes put up 
siother $1,000 this evening that the Senate would 
Sewove My. Johnson. The article in Tux TRIBUNE acquittal of the President, of to-day from the Washington correspondence of a 
Democratic newspaper in Boston, relative to John- 
son’s revealing, on Sunday night, the names of the 
Senators who would vote for his acquittal, increases 
the indignation against Grimes, Fessenden, Trom- 
bull, and Henderson, The Demoerats already claim 
Fessenden, Trumbull, and Grimes as their own. 
A Democratic delegation from Pennsylvania is in 
town to-night in conference with some of the Con- 
servatives and Copperheads discussing the advica- 
bility of running Chief-Justice Chase and Fessenden 
on the Democratic ticket for President and Vice 
President. Mr. Welsh, ex-chairman of the Demo- 
cratic Committee of Philadelphia is running the ma- 
chine, and he talks Chase and Fessendon very 
strong. 
The President’s friends are jubilant over the conrse 
of Grimes, Fessenden, and Trumbull, and are pro- 
fuse in their thanks to Providence that these gen- 
tlemen have at last got their eyes open.

ing was held here last night, and resolutions were unani

political and social execration such Republican Senators

Triumphant Democracy.
The victory is more complete
and decisive than in any political
campaign the country ever had in
a strongly contested election by all
the states of the union.
Mr. Cleveland is elected president
by a majority in the electoral col
lege greater than the entire college
than unanimously elected Wash
ington as the first president of the
United States. The states contrib
uting to this result are from every
part of the union. Once again the
Democracy has shown that it is the
national party and that Republican
ism is sectionalism. New England
is represented in the Cleveland
column. Three of the four Middlt
States are firmly grounded in
Democracy. The South is with the
party, which promised to shield it
from force hill domination. The
west and northwest have shown
that tariff reform was a vital issue
and that McKinleyism and mono
poly were inimical to the farming
sections of the country, while from
tho Pacific slope California again
brings her contribution to the
Democratic cause. Only once has
California failed to give its elec
toral vote to the Republicans since
the civil war was inaugurated. In
1880 that state gave five votes to
Hancock and one to Garfield. Now
California gives nine votes to the
Democracy. The results, how ever,
in Ohio and in the other western
states have been the most surpris
ing and satisfactory.
It would appear that congress is
thoroughly safe to be in accord
with President Cleveland in all
matters of tariff reform and on all
questions of Federal interventions
with the elections in the states.
Even if Democrats and Republicans
should prove to be of equal num
bers in the senate, the Populist
senators would hold the balance of
power. The policy of the People’s
partj T is thoroughly committed in
favor of immediate rectification of
the unjust tariff laws. A judicious
law passed by the house would in
evitably be passed by the senate.
The people of the country have
spoken in such unmistakable terms
that the Republican senators from
the West will break away from
further support of high protective
tariffs. Some of these.Republican
senators have shown their pur
poses in this direction already. It
will be remembered also that Re
publican senators defeated the
passage of the force bill.
Grover Cleveland will finS a con
gress which will give him the sup
port and co-operation to carry out
the plain wishes of the American
people, emphatically expressed.
It is not likeiy that in the matter
of the confirmation of Mr. Cleve
land’s nominations and appoint
ments any factious or party opposi
tion will be made. The Democracy
will take the reins of the govern
ment in all departments next
March under omens the most aus
picious.
What W ould come if Protection
Should Be Abandoned.
_ The London Spectator, specula
ting upon what might follow if the
Democrats should get control of the
Government in the United States,
says:
“If America abandone protection,
her trade, both at home and abroad,
will double in ten years. We see
what her natural resources and the
skill and industray of her people
have done for her in spirt of pro
tection. The impetus her indust
ries will receive under a system of
unrestricted commercial inter
course will create wealth even
beyond the dreams of Wall street.
The effect on the greatest of Ameri
can industries—agriculture—will
be immediate. If America takes
our woolens and cottons and hard
ware in increased quantities she
will at once find the demand for
her corn doubled and trebled. The
fact that the ships that go to fetch
American corn go in ballast is an
immense restriction on the amount
taken. Let them both go full and
return full and American farmers
will find the demand for their corn
very greatly increased. But it must
not be supposed that because we
send more woolens and cottons to
America therefore America will
produce no more of these articles.
She will, under a system of free
trade, be a far greater manufactur
ing country than she is now. After
the first year or two the manufact
urers will find that they can easily-
complete with their European
rivals, and that the supplies of raw
material at their doors and the in
genuity of their people amply com-
peusato for the higher wages they
are called on to pay. Pampered
industries, when the pampering is
withdrawn, soon find their level.
But no sooner will America hold
her own in the manufacturing
world than she will begin to out
strip all rivals. At present the
great neutral markets of the East,
of India and China, are closed to
her because the tariff puts her at a
disadvantage. As soon, however,
as she can enter these markets on
terms of equality she will begin to
beat all rivals. Her geographical
position and her command of raw
material will make it quite impos
sible to undersell her in China,
either in cottons or other dry goods.
How can a manufacturer whose
port of dispatch is Liverpool hope
to sell goods in Canton as cheaply
as a manufacturer whose port is
San Francisco?”

sweeping democratic victory will
be the return of Mrs. Cleveland to

During her former residence there

nation, without regard^ to party.

paratively little experience as a

wealth of native charms. Now she

ber of the white house family will

cal heir to all the petting that has

A Ringing Editorial.
On Tuesday the American peo
ple decided whether democracy or
plutocracy is to reign in this coun
try for the next four years.
Whether the masses or the class
es, the millions or the millionaires
are to rule.
Whether it is to be a government
of the people by the people and
for the people, or government of
aristocrats, by aristocrats and for
aristocrats.
Whether the United States is to
be a republic in reality, or, like the
old republic of Venice, a republic
in name, but in fact an oligarchy
whose dispotic and vicious sway,
“corrupting alike the ruler and
the ruled, was but a mockpry of
those sacred principles which are
alone founded in truth and natural
justice.”
The republican party is the party
of the aristocrats; the democrats
is the party of the masses. The
republican is the party of the mil
lionaires; the democratic is the
party of the millions.
The republicans stand for wealth
and aristocracy. Their supremacy
is the supremacy of the rich and
the rule of the few. Their exer
cise of the power is the sway of an
oligarchy.
The democrats represent the
masses. They stand for the popu
lar principles. Their rule will be
the rule of the masses. It will be
the reign of democracy. It will
be government of the people, by
the people and for the people.
The republican party is the par
ty of millionaire manufacturers
and wealthy protected employers.
It is the party of monopolies, trusts
and combines which have so ruth
lessly preyed upon the people. By
protecting all these it has kept
and hoped to keep itself in power
through the money and votes given
in return for protection.
Republican success would have
been the triumph of all these ele
ments. It would have been four
years more of the policy which
makes the rich richer and the poor
poorer, which oppresses the many
for the benefit of the few, which
takes comforts from the poor to
give luxuries to the rich.
The democratic party is the party
of the poor, the party of working
men, the party of the masses. It
stands for competition against
monopoly, for the rights of em
ployes against the tyrranny of em
ployers, for the interests of the
classes'against the exactions of the
claseeaf^or the.comfortof the many,
against the luxury of the few. Its

CLEVELANDS ADDRESS.
-
The shortest inaugural address, aud
the same time the fullest oue of
solid sense and j dgment ever
by a President was that delivered
by Cleveland at his inauguration
orday last. MU, » «k. ^
two columns in priut it covers every
point and touches upon every ' impor- *
tant political question now before the
American people. In it he elearlv and
; equivocally . ,, advocates , every important
measure proposed by the party which
put him in office and makes valuable
suggestions as to the necessary legis- j
lation to bring about such measures.
lie starts oil by warning the people
against the tendency towards anarchy
and nihilism which has been apparent
recently, and he is right in giving
this question precedence over all oth.
era. It is time we were recognizing it
and setting about to keep it dowu. lie
next advocates a sound and stable cur
reucy and his utterances upon this
momentous queston are so clever that
no faction of the party nor interest of
the people can feel than tbeir rights
are jeopardized. He is sound on this
question. lie follows this with con¬
demnation of the spirit now rife to ex¬
pect from the government especial
and direct individual advantages.
This leads him to tariff protection
which he cotidems in no unmistakable
term, declaring it the bane of republi¬
can institutions and the eon-tant peril
of our government. Of it he well
says: “It prevents the patriotic senti¬
ment of our countrymen, and tempts
them to the pitiful calculation of sor
ded grain to be derived from their gov¬
ernments maintenance. It undermines
the self-reliance of our people, and
substitutes in its place dependence
upon governmental favoritism and
stupifies every enabling trait of Ameri¬
can citizenship." Were truer words
ever uttered.
lie is as outspoken on the pension
question and characterizes such waste
of public money as crime against the
citizens, and declares that she strength
and sturdiness of our national charac¬
ter is thereby sapped. Ilis declaration
upon civil service reform must have
been witheiing to the horde of office
seekers who heard him. Fitness for
the duties to be performed is made his
standard and that is what it should be.
Monopolies and combines come in
for a large share of censure in the ad¬
dress. They are declared great me¬
naces to the interests of the people and
declared unnatural and opposed to
American sense of fairness, and
federal powers are enjoined to
the people from their interference and
exactions.
More space in the address is
to the tariff than any other
discussed by it. lie positively
that “ihe people of the United
have decreed that the control of
government in its legislative aud
utive branches shall lie given to the
litical party pledged in the most posi¬
tive terms to the accomplishment
tariff reform. They have thus
mined in favor of a more just and
table system of federal taxation.
President’s utterances upon this
question leave no doubts as to
will be his policy, and the people
expect the day to soon dawn when
will be relived of the robber tariff
which is depressing the country
large.
He closes the address with an
nest appeal to those whom the
have chosen to work together for
redemption of the pledges the
has made to the people. lie
that all petty dissections in the
and all personal preferences of its
ficials be laid aside and that most sin¬
cere, harmonious and disinterested
forts be put forth for the good of the
nation as a nation.
All in all it is an address characteristic
of the grand man and statesman, Gro¬
ver Cleveland. He makes no empty
promises only consecrates himself to
tin.: will of the people as that will has
been expressed. This address is calcu¬
lated to inspire more confidence in him,
and make his subjects feel that their
interests are safe in his hands. Long
live Cleveland.
The greatest of them all was the in¬
augural ceremonies last Saturday,
aud they put in office the greatest
President of them all, save, perhaps,
Washington,

The exhibitions by the famous Prof,

rick. They remain here next week and
will exhibit in different churches.
A very interesting revival is going on
Bt Mt. Tabor Baptist church, Rev. N.
H. M hitmire, pastor. During the week
he has be* n ably assisted by Rev P. J

“Under the Palms.”
The above is the title of the Canta t
to be given by St. Stephen’s Sund a
School on Thursday evening, April 22d
at Odd Fellow’s Hall on Harris St.
It “represents the return of the Jew
from captivity in Babylon during the
reign of Cyrus ; the repose of the pil
grims on their way to Jerusalem by
Ahava river where they prayed to God
for future direction for themselves and
‘their little ones’ ; and the triumphant
celebration of the feast of the tabernacles
after their return”.
It is fully three years since this
Sunday-School has made any bid for
public patronage in this line, and it is
hoped that the friends of this congrega
tion may join heartily with them in
ensuring the success of this beautiful
musical treat.
To Sing Two Evenings.
The famous and renowned Miss Flora
Batson as has already been mantioned,
will be in the city and will sing at the
different churches during next week.
Lt is needless for us to undertake to de
scribe to the public the sweetness of the
voice of this lady. Those who heard her
when she was in Savannah before know
how she captivated all who had the op
portunity of heaiing her, and there is
no doubt that she will do equally as
well or better this time.
On Monday evening next she will ap
pear at the Second Baptist church and
on Thursday evening at B th-Eden
church. It is hoped that the public
will avail themselves of this very excel
lent opportunity to hear one of the great
est singers in the world.
A Grand Concert.
The concert given under the man
agement of Mrs. R. C. Smith at and for
the benefit of College Park Baptist
church on Monday night was up to the
standard. AU of the participants ren
dered their parts very creditably and
deserve much commendation.
“Alfred the Great” was well rendered
in four acts, and brought forth much
applause. A large number of persons
from the city attended.
It is hoped that it was a success
financially.
Rev. R. H. Thomas, the pastor and
the members are highly pleased with
the patronage they received.
This church is rapidily coming to
the front in every particular.
Mr. N. J. Williams Dead.
Mr. Newton J Williams died at his
residence 121 Arnold street last week
after a short illness. Mr. Williams was
an exempliary Christian, always endeav
oring to be of service to humanity. He
leaves two sons, Mr. W. G and David
Williams one daughter, Mrs. Mary
Childs, to mourn his loss.
Mr. Williams was a native of South
Carolina but has been residing in this
city for several years. He was an earnest
and faithful member of the Second
Baptist church.

There was a iiveTy debate at the
Beach Institute on Friday last at the
meeting of the Washburn Lyceum
a society formed of the students of the
ninth and tenth grades. The partici
pants in the debate were Messrs: Chas.
Noble and Cyrus Camjfield. The sub
ject debated was, “Resolvd, That the
Rights of Sufferase be Granted to Wo
men’’ Affirm. Mr. Chas. Nobles, Neg
Mr. Cyrus Campfield. After a lively
debate the judges rendered their de
cision in favor of the affirmative,
Washington Notes.
The beautiful inaugural day on which
Pres. McKinley took the oath of office
still continues to be a subj ct of talk
and will ever be remembered as a most
remarkable occurrence. For days pre
seeding the 4th, the weather was un
prepossessing, cloudy and with almost
continual rain ; and thousands of dollars
had been expended to secure the occu
pants of reviewing stands from the cold
blasts anticipated, but on the morning
of the eventful day, as if by a special
dispensation of Providence, the sun
shone forth in all its glory and by 12
o’clock it was an ideal spring day and so
continued to the close of the ceremonies
at night. But on the next day and for
many days succeeding it was as miser
able weather as ever experienced by the
old inhabitants.
The people devoutly hope that the
event may be considered and wid be an
omen of the success of the administra
tion with the attendant prosperity of
the people.
The parade was one of the most bril
liant ever witnessed in Washington and
was frequently cheered by the enthu
siastic thousands of pe^p e on the line
of march.
Now that the crowds that name to
witness the inauguration have returned
home, the politicians are taking charge
of the city, though there is but little to
be accomplished except in emergency
cases, as the various departments are
not organized with friends of the Re
publican administration. The president
is very cautious in selecting his men,
and consequently appointments have
been made very slowly.
Congress will meet on Monday and it
is expected to at once pass a tariff law
in harmony with ihe views of the
administration without any unnecessary
delay.
Georgia is now represented at the
Capitol by Messrs. A. E Buck, J W.
Lyons. J H. Deveaux, C. C. Wimbish,
W. A. Pledger T. H. Malone, Jackson
McHenry, W. F. Corbett, C. H. Adam-
mr, Il T. Furr, H. A. Hagler, H. P.
Farrow, Tom Blodget and others. Mr.
J. B. Deveaux is in his glory having so
many of the Georgia boys in the city
whom he always delights to entertain.
Major Lee Given His Liberty.
Major Lee, the colored man who has
been illegally confined in the peniten
tiary for the past fourteen years, was
released from custody on Monday. He
was charged with the murder of a white
man. The evidence against him was
only circumstantial. He was convicted,
a new trial was ordered but never given
and all these years he has been confined
at hard labor. Is not the state liable
for this? Lee should endeavor to get
legal advice.

ing of the hymn which Presi

gation arose and joined in the last

It was after 3 o’clock tvhen the silent
and anxious throngs outside the church
saw the solemn pageant reappear

Out Tuscarawas street the long pro
cession moved through a section of the

ed the same sorrow-stricken aspect

THE JOURNEY ENDED.
Life neplete With Worldly Honors
nnd the Love of Hio Fellow Men,
His Newly Mmle Grave Annointed
With a People,* Tear*.
Cemetery, Canton, 0., Sept. 19.—The
line of the funeral march from the
church to the cemetery was about one
and one-half miles In length. For
hours even before the time set for the
commencement of the funeral exercises
at the McKinley home, the streets
along the entire length of the line of
march were crowded with spectators.
From the gates of the cemetery to the
.doors of the church there was on each
side, of the street an almost unbroken
line of soldiers, and on all the inter
secting streets, detachments of the
militia were posted about 100 feet from
the line of march.
It was exactly 4:04 o’clock when the
funeral car bore the remains of the
dead President through the gateway of
his last resting place. Twenty minutes
after that time the brief services at
the vault were over, the members of
the family and the distinguished men
of the nation who had come so far to
do him honor, had passed through the
gates on their homeward way. One
hour and forty minutes after the hearse
had entered the cemetery, the place
was clear and the dead President was
resting alone under the watchful
care of men of the regu
lar army. A sentry’s measured
treqd resounded from the cement
wafk before the vault, another kept
vigil on the grassy slope above and at
tlie>: head and at the foot of the casket
stood armed men. Before the door,
which was not closed to-night, was
pitched the tent of the guard, and
there it will remain until the doors
are closed to-morrow. Sentries will then
guard the vault every hour of the day
and night until the body has been
borne to its final resting place.
For: nearly an hour before the head
of the funeral procession arrived at
the gate of the cemetery, the strains
of the dirges played by the bands came
over the hilltop to the watchers by
the vault, telling them that the pro
cession was on its way. Behind the
Canton band came the Grand Army
posts, fully 500 of the veterans march
ing by. As they passed along the flow
er-strewn path, many of them were
weeping bitterly, and they stopped by
dozens to gather the blossoms which
lay at their feet, and carried them
away as mementoes. The sweet pea
blossoms that were scattered along the
road were the offering of the school
children of Nashville, Tenn., and no
tribute of love that was seen during
the funeral exercises more amply ful
filled its mission or more completely
carried its message of affection. To
night hundreds of the blossoms are in
the possession of the marchers in the
parade, and are held by spectators who
came into the cemetery after the close
of the parade to carry them away.
From the first carriage that stopped
at the foot of the walk leading up to
the vault, President Roosevelt and
Commander Cowles of the navy, alight
ed. The President walked slowly to
ward the vault and took a position on
the south side of the walk close to the
door. As Secretary Root came up the
walk he assumed a similar position
on the north side of the walk, and the
other members of the cabinet ranged
themselves by the side of the Presi
dent and Secretary of War. With
bared heads the President and mem
bers of the cabinet, who were followed
by the officers of the army and navy,
stood on each side of the walk, the
lines reaching just to the edge of the
roadway. Within a minute after the
formation of the lines, the funeral car
came up to the walk. The coffin was
gently lifted from the hearse and
borne to the door of the vault, where
It rested upon the catafalque.
Bishop Joyce of Minneapolis, read
the burial service of the Methodist
Church, slowly, but in a voice that
could be plainly heard by all who were
grouped around the vault. As his
words ended there was a brief pause,
for it had been understood that a
quartette of the Knights Templar was
to be present to render a hymn.
Through a misunderstanding, however.
it had not arrived and after satisfying
himself of this fact. Col. Bingham
waved his hand to eight buglers of
the Canton band who had taken
station upon the side of the
mound above, and to the
south of the vault. Instantly
from the eight bugles rang out the
notes of the soldier's last call, "taps.”
It was beautifully done, and the last
notes of the bugles died away so soft
ly that all who heard it remained list
ening for a few seconds to hear if It
was really ended. When the last note
had floated away, Secretary Wilson was
in tears, Secretary Hitchcock was al
most weeping and the President was
gazing grimly at the walk. Ic was
the last moment for the men who had
been so closely associated with the
President for so long, and the thought
seemed greater than most of them
could bear. It was all ended at last,
and Capt. Biddle of Company C of the
Fourteenth Infantry, who will com
mand the guard which is to be placed
around the vault, stationed sentries at
the head and foot of the casket and
in front of the vault.
The President, the members of the
cabinet and the officers of the army
and navy then entered their carriages,
and. followed by the members of the
family, pased out qf the cemetery and
returned to the city. The delay caused
by the services at the vault being over,
te procession resumed its march. Every
mail In the line save those tn uhtform,
who rendered appropriate honor In
other ways, went to the casket with
uncovered head. As the head of the
division containing the Knights Temp
lar wheeled Into the cemetery,
the quartette that had been
delayed In reaching the place
x for the previous ceremonies took up *
position to the south of the vault and
sang "Farewell My Brother.” This
hymn was followed by others, includ
ing "Rock of Ages,” "The Christian’s
Good Night." and "The Wayside
Cross.” The selections were beautiful
ly rendered and no part of the funeral
ceremonies in Canton was more im
pressive. The darkness was gathering
fast as the Knights sang on, and many
in the multitude around the casket
were moved to tears, and the sound of
sobs was distinctly audible in the crowd
that lined the fence beyond the line
of National Guardsmen.
The last of the procession passed the
bier at 5:45 and then orders were given
by Capt. Biddle that the cemetery
should be cleared. The order was quick
ly carried out and the President was
left in the care of his guard of honor.
MRS. M’KINLEY’ifBRAVERY.
The Dear Lady'* Mental Suffering In
tense, lint Her Fortitude Triumphs.
Canton, 0., Sept. 19.—The friends of
Mrs. McKinley do not to-night regard
her as being upon the verfee of a col
lapse. On the contrary they express
themselves as quite confident that she
will be spared to them for a long time
in at least as good a state of health
as she has enjoyed for the last five
years. Dr. Rixey said late this morn
ing:
"Mrs. McKinley is bearing up as well
as could be expected under the circum
stances. She is suffering Intensely
from her bereavement and has fre
quently given way to sobs and tears,
but for all that she has been doing as
well as any woman could do under
similar circumstances. We have no im
mediate expectations of anything but
favorable conditions in her case.”
“SIMPLY DID MY DUTY.”
So Say* A**a**ln Cnlgnnz in Speak
ing of III* Horrible Deed.
Buffalo, .Sept. 20. —The Courier this
morning prints the following:
"What’s the use of talking about
that? I killed the President. I am an
anarchist, and simply did my duty;
that’s all I’ll say."
Leon F. Czolgosz, the assassin cf
President McKinley said these words
to Frank A. Olozanowski, editor of
Buffaloski, a Polish newspaper. Olo
zanowski paid his second visit to the
assassin’s cell. He was sent by the
district attorney in pursuance of vain
efforts to move the prisoner’s stubborn
tongue.
“Czolgosz talked freely on every
subject which I suggested, except his
crime,” said Olozanowski to-night.
"His conversation would have been
entertaining coming from a man other
than the President’s assassin. He talk
ed on the Polish alliance and a variety
of other subjects, hut when I spoke of
his crime he merely said:
“ ’What’s the use of talking about
that? I killed the President. I am an
anarchist. I simply did my duty.
That’s all I’ll say.'
“Czolgosz spoke earnestly and deter
minedly. I tried him several ways,
but he would not add a word to his
declaration. I don’t believe anyone has
any more from him about the crime.
Czolgosz is intelligent and I don’t be
lieve he will tell more.”
The closest watch is being kept on
Czolgosz, and the sheriff’s officers also
seem bent on aiding the prosecution by
getting admissions from the prisoner,
but so far they have been unsuccess
ful. The prisoner talks on ordinary
subjects occasionally, but will not talk
on subjects leading up to assassi
nation of the President. When ques
tioned in that relation Czolgosz re
sumes his stubborn silence.
DESERVED RECOGNITION.
Ir. Rixey Receives Appointment ns
Naval Surgeon General.
Canton, 0., Sept. 19. —President
Roosevelt has informed Mrs. McKinley,
through Secreatry Cortelyou, that in
pursuance of the intention of the late
President McKinley, and in recogni
tion of devoted services, as well as
because of eminent fitness, Medical In
spector P. M. Rixey will be appointed
surgeon general of the navy upon the
expiration of the term of Surgeon Gen
eral Van Reypen.
The Secretary of the Navy has been
requested by the President to instruct
Dr. Rixey to make such arrangements
as will enable him to continue, if need
ed, his care for Mrs. McKinley between
now and the time of his appointment
to his new position.
WILL CONVENESCHLEY COURT
Expectation That Admiral Dewey
Will Call llody to Order To-day.
Washington, Sept. 19.—With the ex
pectation that the Schley court of In
quiry will resume its sessions at 11
o'clock to-morrow, Messrs. Wilson and
Rayner were busy at Admiral Schley's
headquarters to-day preparing to
take up their duties as counsel in be
half of Admiral Schley. Admiral
Dewey, who is at Canton with the
funeral party, will be in Washington
to-morrow morning In time to call the
court to order at the hour named.
WOOD IN WASHINGTON.
Culm's Governor General Will Con
fer With I’resiilenl Hoosevelt.
Washington, Sept. 19. —Gen. Leonard
Wood, Governor of Cuba, accompanied
by his secretary, Alex Gonzales, ar
rived in Washington late to-night.
While here he will have conferences
with President Roosevelt and with Sec
retary Root and will start on his re
turn trip Saturday.
The business which calls Gen. Wood
to the city at this time is to submit
to Secretry Root the new electoral law
adopted by the constitutional conven
tion.
Gn. Wood thinks there should be
some action by the American govern
ment towards the relief of the sugar
planters and tobacco producers, who
are now handicapped, he said, by the
duties on Imports of their products in
to the United States. A moderate re
duction in the tariff on both these
products, sugar first and tobacco sec
ond. he said, was absolutely essential
to the future welfare of the island.
Gen. Wood will earnestly recommend
the reduction of the tariff on these two
products.

Bernard Weissman was a one-man committee, although several contributors paid for the ad's placement; they included future Dallas Cowboys owner H.R. "Bum" Bright and businessman Nelson Bunker Hunt (the son of billionaire oil man H.L. Hunt). - Gary Mack, Curator
Chuck Altman, originally from Massachusetts, knew conservative activist Bernard Weissman.  In his 2010 oral history with The Sixth Floor Museum, Altman describes how the two men served in the U.S. Army together in Germany in 1962.  

Altman shared memories of the secret political meetings that Weissman and others held: "One of the gentlemen in the group was an MP, a military policeman, and he would always stand guard... because the group was meeting illegally, and he would make sure that nobody would discover what they were doing. And so, I subsequently found out what their intent was--and their primary intent was to elect Barry Goldwater as president in 1964 and make sure that John Kennedy was removed from office...  But their underpinning was what bothered me because they were passionate to a degree that was concerning because they would say, 'by any means possible.' And so, as they kept going, they would say, 'Now, here's what we're going to do...  We are all going to go to Dallas.' Why Dallas, as opposed to any other city?  Because it is a conservative headquarters, if you will, in the United States... And they said, 'We are going to take up positions, business positions, here in the Dallas area that will enable us to achieve political influence. And Chuck... we will facilitate your getting a job on The Dallas Morning News, and we guarantee that this will earn you $50,000 a year.'"  

Chuck Altman ultimately declined the group's offer and was living in Massachusetts at the time of the assassination.  He was shocked when he learned that Weissman's name was attached to the infamous Morning News advertisement that appeared on the day of the assassination. - Stephen Fagin, Curator

End full text (scraped):
WASHINGTON, Aug. 8, 1974 (UPI)-Richard M. Nixon told Gerald R. Ford Thursday that he is resigning as the 37th president of the United States, and Ford will be sworn in as the new president Friday at 6 p.m. EDT, UPI learned.
With Nixon facing almost certain removal from office by Congress under the impeachment process because of Watergate, Nixon called in Ford just before noon, Washington time.
A high White House official said Nixon formally gave Ford the word that he is stepping down.
The White House said Nixon would go on nationwide radio and television at 9 p.m. EDT Thursday. In the speech, he will tell the American people and the world of his decision to become the first president to quit office before his term expired, the official said.
House Democratic Leader Thomas P. O'Neill said he had learned that Nixon would announce he is prepared to hand his letter of resignation to Secretary of State Henry A. Kissinger as provided by law.
After conferring with Nixon for 70 minutes, Ford called Kissinger and they talked for 10 minutes, according to a Kissinger aide. They arranged to meet in Ford's office next to the White House at mid-afternoon. Ford canceled a scheduled trip to the West, including a stop in Portland, Ore.
Nixon was under extreme pressure from his own party members and friends in Congress to quit following the disclosure Monday that contrary to his past statements, he tried to limit an FBI investigation of Watergate and was told of possible high-level involvement in the scandals as early as June 23, 1972.
Asked how Ford has taken the news, Paul Miltich, the vice president's press secretary, said, "He's remarkably calm and his mood is one of business-like dispatch."
Ford met with top aides and advisers to discuss the inaugural address he will deliver from the East Room late Friday, sources said. He also began working on contingency plans for selecting his vice president and for forming his Cabinet, according to the sources.
Nixon decided to give up his fight for survival after being told that the House was certain to approve articles of impeachment against him, possibly with only a handful of "no" votes, and that he might be able to command about 15 votes in a Senate trial. He would need 34 to avert a two-thirds margin needed for conviction.
Press Secretary Ronald L. Ziegler, one of the few remaining top aides who had been with Nixon since he first took office Jan. 20, 1969, announced to a crowd of reporters after the Nixon-Ford meeting that the President would be going on television.
The effective time of the resignation was left uncertain. Indications were that it might be effective at the time Ford takes the oath as 38th president.
Sen. Edward W. Brooke, R-Mass., introduced a resolution in the Senate urging that federal, state or local prosecutors refrain from prosecuting Nixon for Watergate wrongdoing, but said he would not vote for it himself "if the President says tonight 'I am not guilty' and goes about the country afterward saying, 'I have been martyred, I have been hounded out of office.'"
Crowds of curious, silent passersby peered through the black iron fence surrounding the White House, awaiting the final climactic word or Nixon's fate. Aides said the White House switchboard had been jammed for nearly two days with telephone calls about the resignation rumors that have swept Washington and the world.
On Wall Street, the stock market opened Thursday with another leap upward in anticipation of a Nixon resignation, after the Dow Jones industrial average posted a 23.78-point gain Wednesday, the third largest of the year. Speculation over Nixon's departure saw the U.S. dollar rise and the price of gold decline on European money markets.
Ford began the day Thursday by assuming traditional presidential duty of awarding posthumous Medals of Honor to the families of seven U.S. military men slain in Southeast Asia.
At Nixon's request, he left the ceremony at Blair House and crossed Pennsylvania Avenue to see Nixon.
The entire Nixon family, including the President's daughters, Julie and Tricia and their husbands, waited at the White House.  One aide said Mrs. Nixon and her daughters, especially Julie, were adamant in urging Nixon to remain in office and fight impeachment in the House and Senate.
Ford, who stood on the brink of becoming the 38th president of the United States when the day began, refused all comment to reporters after the awards ceremony which Defense Secretary James Schlesinger also attended.
Asked if he would serve in a Ford administration, Schlesinger replied curtly:  "That is a decision which Mr. Ford would have to make."
There were widespread reports-not denied by Ford's aides-that the vice president began making transition plans Monday night, the same day Nixon released three previously withheld tapes disclosing he had approved and taken part in the Watergate cover-up as early as June 23, 1972, within a week of the break-in at Democratic national headquarters.
Former Defense Secretary Melvin R. Laird, a close and influential political associate of Ford's, has urged that he nominate former Gov. Nelson A. Rockefeller of New York as his vice president. There were unconfirmed reports that Rockefeller had been sounded out and had indicated he is willing to accept.
It would be the first time in history that the United States had both a president and a vice president who were not elected.
Brook's "sense of the Senate" resolution, which would have no binding effect on the Justice Department or Watergate special prosecutor Leon Jaworski, was immediately opposed by Democratic leaders.
"I don't want the President to feel that he was driven out of office," said Senate Democratic Leader Mike Mansfield.  Mansfield reflected an apparent consensus of fellow Democrats that any congressional granting of immunity-which would be of dubious constitutional value anyway-would be viewed as pressure on Nixon to resign.
As for impeachment proceedings in Congress, scheduled to begin Aug. 19 on the House floor, Speaker Carl Albert said he opposed going any further if Nixon resigns. He and other House leaders implicitly rejected a suggestion by Mansfield that impeachment proceedings should continue, including a Senate trial, to "lay it all out and get away from the idea that the President was being force out."
Sen. Barry M. Goldwater of Arizona, the Senate's most highly respected Republican conservative who saw Nixon Wednesday, said the President could not count on more than 15 Senate votes-far short of the 34 needed to escape conviction and removal from office.
Ford originally had been scheduled to leave Washington at noon for a Republican fund-raising telethon in Los Angeles Thursday night.  GOP National Chairman George Bush postponed the event indefinitely because "the fast-changing situation in Washington has made it uncertain that party leaders could come to Los Angeles to appear on the program."

End headline / topic line (verbatim): Nation: Not Yes Men

Start full text (scraped):
WASHINGTON, Jan. 20, 1977 (UPI)-Jimmy Carter assumed the awesome burdens of the presidency of the United States today and asked Americans to join him in a renewed search for "humility, mercy and justice."
The Georgia dirt farmer, who rose from political obscurity a few years ago to the nation's highest office, became the 39th president in a simple ceremony almost as old as the country.
In a populist inaugural speech to a crowd of nearly 150,000 jammed in front of the Capitol and a national television audience, the 52-year-old President said his administration and the nation's third century would be marked with "a new beginning...a new dedication...a new spirit."
"A President may sense and proclaim that new spirit," he said without the famous Carter smile, "but only a people can provide it."
Chief Justice Warren Burger administered the oath to Carter at 12:03 p.m., just minutes after House Speaker Thomas P. O'Neill swore in the Minnesotan Walter Mondale as the nation's 42nd vice president.
His right hand on the Bible given him by his mother, the first Democratic president in eight years intoned the same 35-word oath of every chief executive since George Washington in 1789-swearing to "preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the United States."
Although forecasters had predicted clouds and possible snow flurries, it was a brilliant winter day and a bright sun shone down on the white marble Capitol and colorful throng outside.
Carter wore a topcoat to guard against the chilly 25-degree temperatures, but outgoing President Ford and Vice President Rockefeller were among the few wearing only suits. Ford joked good naturedly about wearing long underwear.
Ford, who was praised by Carter in his speech for "all he has done to heal our land," flew off to California and retirement immediately after the ceremony.
Despite the cold, thousands packed along Pennsylvania Avenue to watch the new President return to the White House and witness the 2 1/2 hour inaugural parade with its 400 horses, floats from every state in the union and 15,000 marchers.
Carter was surrounded by his wife, Rosalyn, his mother "Miss Lillian," brother Billy, three sons and 9-year-old daughter Amy-all members of the clan who campaigned tirelessly for a year on his upset road to the White House.
"You have given me a great responsibility, to stay close to you, to be worthy of you, and to exemplify what you are," he said.
"Let us learn together and laugh together and work together and pray together-confident that in the end we will triumph together."
Carter's address broke no new ground. It was a philosophical speech, gentle in tone but firm in its commitment to human dignity and the American dream. He urged moderation in striving for that dream-lest the natural resources of a great nation be drained.
"More is not necessarily better," he declared.
He rattled no sabers, but said U.S. military strength must be "so sufficient that it need never be proven in combat."
He pledged that America will be "ever vigilant and never vulnerable, and we will fight our wars against poverty, ignorance and injustice...for those are the enemies against which our forces can be honorably marshaled."
Carter, as is his habit, awakened early on his big day. With President Ford at his side, Carter rode in a black limousine up Pennsylvania Avenue, "The Avenue of Presidents," to the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration ceremony.
Yet to come before the rigorous day culminated at last in the White House were the 2 1/2 hour parade back down Pennsylvania Avenue to the White House, and the seven gala parties expected to draw 60,000 celebrants.
Ironically, it was two years ago today that Carter launched his million-to-one presidential bid with a speech in Baton Rouge, La.
Then he was "Jimmy Who?"
Now he is "Mr. President."
He pledged that under his leadership, the United States will move this year "a step toward our ultimate goal-the elimination of all nuclear weapons from this earth."
"We urge all other people to join us, for success can mean life instead of death."
Carter pledged the "perseverance and wisdom" of his nation "to limit the world's armaments to those necessary to each nation's own domestic safety."
He enumerated what he said were not his goals alone, but the "common hopes" of the nation he will lead into a third century.
And he said that if the goals were met, it would not be his triumph "but the affirmation of our nation's continuing moral strength and our belief in an undiminished, ever-expanding American dream."
Among the things for which he hoped his presidency would be remembered were:
A "renewed search for humility, mercy and justice."
"That we had torn down the barriers that separated those of different race and religion...and where there had been mistrust, built unity, with a respect for diversity."
"Productive work"' for everyone able to perform it.
A strengthening of the American family-"the basis of our society."
"Respect for the law, and equal treatment under the law, for the weak and the powerful, the rich and the poor."
"And that we had enabled our people to be proud of their own government once again."
Carter noted that two centuries ago, America's birth was "a milestone in the long quest for freedom."
"But the bold and brilliant dream which excited the founders of our nation still awaits its consummation. I have no new dream to set forth today, but rather urge a fresh faith in the old dream."
"The American dream endures," he said. "We must once again have full faith in our country-and in one another.
"I believe America can be better."
He urged the nation to let its "recent mistakes bring a resurgent commitment to the basic principles of our nation, for we know that if we despise our own government we have no future."
He said Americans "have already found a high degree of personal liberty, and we are now struggling to enhance equality of opportunity," but he warned:
"Our commitment to human rights must be absolute...The powerful must not persecute the weak, and human dignity must be enhanced."
